items.title: Web Security 101: Seeing the Real Difference with HTTPS and HSTS
items.author: Okan Yenigün
items.content:
Encrypt Everything: The Practical Beginner’s Guide to HTTPS and HSTS

When building your first web app, it’s tempting to start with simplicity. You might initially use plain HTTP to communicate between your web server and the browser. But doing this leaves your data completely exposed.

With plain HTTP, all data transferred is sent openly, in clear text. Anyone on the network can read or even modify this data.

HTTP sends data in clear text

HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) transmits data as plain text without encryption. An attacker can use simple network-monitoring tools to intercept and read this data easily.

You          Internet           Website[User] ----------------> [Attacker] ----------------> [Server]
       (username: alice, password: 1234)
[User] --- username: alice, password: 1234 ---> [Attacker can read it] ---> [Website]HTTP sends data in clear text

Let’s do a quick hands-on experiment.

In your terminal, create a basic HTML login form and start a Python HTTP server:

mkdir http-demo && cd http-demo
echo '<form action="/login" method="post">
Username: <input type="text" name="username"><br>
Password: <input type="password" name="password"><br>
<button type="submit">Login</button>
python3 -m http.server 8000

Run tcpdump to monitor HTTP traffic in another terminal:

sudo tcpdump -i lo0 -A port 8000

You’ll see output similar to:

POST /login HTTP/1.1Host: localhost:8000Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
username=testuser&password=mypassword

Notice the credentials appear clearly in plain text.

This makes HTTP completely unsuitable for any sensitive or authenticated interactions.

Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure

HTTPS is simply HTTP wrapped with TLS (Transport Layer Security) encryption.

TLS encrypts the data traveling between the client (browser) and the web server, preventing anyone from reading or modifying that data in transit.

When your browser connects to a website using HTTPS, the following steps (known as a TLS Handshake) happen behind the scenes:

The client (browser) contacts the server, proposing encryption methods (cipher suites) and TLS version.

2. Server Hello & Certificate

The server chooses an encryption method, sends back its digital certificate (issued by a Certificate Authority), and proves its identity.

The client verifies the certificate’s authenticity against trusted Certificate Authorities.

4. Establishing Encryption Keys

Both client and server generate shared encryption keys securely using asymmetric cryptography (public/private keys).

5. Secure Communication Established

From this point onward, all transmitted data is encrypted and safe from interception.

Let’s convert the previous example into HTTPS.

First, make a simple login form again.

echo '<form action="/login" method="post">  Username: <input name="username"><br>
  Password: <input name="password" type="password"><br>
  <button type="submit">Login</button>
python3 -m http.server 8000

Observe traffic with tcpdump

sudo tcpdump -i lo0 -A port 8000

Install mkcert and generate certificates

brew install mkcert         # macOSmkcert -install
mkcert localhost 127.0.0.1 ::1

This creates localhost+2.pem and localhost+2-key.pem.

Serve the same page with HTTPS using http-server

/Users/okanyenigun/.npm-global/bin/http-server -S -C localhost+2.pem -K localhost+2-key.pem
Available on:  https://localhost:8080

Submit credentials again (the form works the same as before).

This time, you will NOT see your username and password in readable form. The data is encrypted and appears as random-looking characters.

After serving your page over HTTPS, you should see a lock icon in the address bar.

Click the lock. You’ll see information about the secure connection and the certificate.

HTTP Strict Transport Security

So, you’ve got HTTPS working. That’s good. But there’s still a subtle risk: a user could accidentally visiting your site over plain HTTP first. This is where HSTS comes in.

HSTS is a browser security policy that tells browser:

For this site, never use plain HTTP again — always use HTTPS, every single time, no matter what.

Once a browser sees this instruction from your site, it will:

  • Automatically upgrade all future connections to HTTPS
  • Block connections if HTTPS is not available
  • Protect users from “SSL stripping” attacks, where attackers try to force connections to use insecure HTTP

To enable HSTS, your server needs to send this response header:

Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload
  • max-age=63072000: How long (in seconds) browsers should remember to only use HTTPS.
  • includeSubDomains: Also apply this rule to all subdomains.
  • preload: Requests your site to be added to browsers’ built-in HSTS lists.

Even if your main page is loaded over HTTPS, if you reference assets (scripts, images, CSS, iframes) over plain HTTP, browsers will block or warn about those “mixed content” resources.

If your browser’s console, you’ll see errors or warnings like:

Mixed Content: The page at 'https://example.com' was loaded over HTTPS, but requested an insecure image 'http://example.com/logo.png'.
  • Always use https:// for all resources.
  • If you control the assets, serve everything over HTTPS.
  • If you use third-party assets that aren’t available via HTTPS, find alternatives or self-host them.

Forgetting to Renew Certificates

HTTPS certificates have an expiration date (Let’s Encrypt is usually 90 days). If you forget to renew, your site suddenly breaks and users see scary warnings.

To avoid, automate renewals. For example, set up a cron job.

TLS relies on accurate system clocks. If your server’s time drifts (clock skew), browsers and clients will refuse to connect, complaining about expired or not-yet-valid certificates.

To avoid, always run time sync (NTP) on your server.

Securing your web app with HTTPS and HSTS isn’t optional, it’s a standard.

Remember to upgrade all resources to HTTPS, automate certificate renewals, and use tools like mkcert for local development.

items.title: Can You Bypass text.length > 20? Hackers Can!
items.author: Dilli Babu Kadati
items.content:
A small mistake in validation. A massive risk in production.

I’m going to explain a common bug that’s often found in many backend servers — and it can have a serious impact on your production environment.

In fact, it can even lead to a attack on your server.

Let’s dive straight into it.

Take a look at the simple Node.js API code below:

const express = require("express");const app = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;
app.post("/api/text", (req, res) => {
  try {
    const { text } = req.body;
      return res.status(400).json({
        error: "Text length cannot exceed 20 characters",
      });      message: `You said: ${text}`,  } catch (error) {
    console.error("Error processing request:", error);
    res.status(500).json({
      error: "Internal server error",
    });});  console.log(`Server is running on port ${PORT}`);
  console.log(`API endpoint: http://localhost:${PORT}/api/text`);
});

We have an API endpoint /api/text that expects a text field in the request body and responds with:

Seems pretty simple and straightforward, right?Well — head over to the comments section and let me know if you spot any bugs in the code above.

Didn’t find anything? No problem — let me explain exactly where the bug is.

Take a closer look at the section of code below:

const { text } = req.body;      return res.status(400).json({
        error: "Text length cannot exceed 20 characters",
      });

Here, we’re extracting the text value from the request body and storing it in a variable named text.Then, we’re checking its length — and if it exceeds 20 characters, we return an error response.

Let me start the server and show you how it works.

When input is less than 20 characters.input:    "text":"Hello World"{
    "message": "You said: Hello World"
}
When input is more than 20 characters.
Input:    "text":"Hello World Hello World Hello World Hello World Hello World"{
    "error": "Text length cannot exceed 20 characters"
}

The code works as expected: ✅ It returns a success response when the text length is ❌ It returns an error response when the text length

🚨 Now let’s see how hackers can bypass this length check.

🔍 Exploiting the .length Check

To understand the trick, we first need to understand how .length works in JavaScript.

In JavaScript, length is a that exists on strings and arrays.When you call .length on a string or array, it returns the number of characters or items in that data structure.

For String
console.log(`Length: ${'Hello world'.length}`)
output: Length: 11console.log(`Length: ${['Hello', 'world'].length}`)

What about other types like and 

let sampleObject={ 'key':"Hello"}console.log(`Length: ${sampleObject.length}`)
Output:    Length: undefined

When you access .length on an , JavaScript simply tries to read a property named length on that object.Since sampleObject doesn’t have a length property defined, it returns undefined.

There’s no error — just a missing property.

💡 So, what if we manually add a fake That’s exactly what hackers exploit.

🧨 Now, let’s see how a hacker uses this behavior to exploit your server.

Stay tuned — we’re about to bypass that seemingly harmless .length > 20 check.

Now, let’s see how uses this to exploit your server.

Hacker Sends Object as value to the text variable. Input:    "text":{
        "value":"asdgsdfsdfsdfsdff",
        "length":10}{
    "message": "You said: [object Object]"
}{        "value":"Hello World",    }{
    "error": "Text length cannot exceed 20 characters"
}

As you can see, we manually set a custom length property on the object — and the server blindly read it. That’s how we successfully bypassed the length check.

By setting length: 10, the server assumed the input was safe and returned a success response.

Now, in our example, we’re simply returning the text. But in real-world applications, the server might:

  • Store the text in a database
  • Use it in a prompt (e.g., for an LLM)
  • Modify or execute it dynamically

All of these could lead to  — including , , or even .

The fix is simple: validate the type of the input before using it.

Instead of just checking the length, first ensure it’s a :

    if (!text || typeof text !== "string") {      return res.status(400).json({
        error: "Text is required and must be a string",
      });

This ensures that your server doesn’t fall for fake .length values on objects and only processes safe, expected input.

Input:{    "length": 10,      "toString": "asdasdasdasdasdasdasdasdasd"  }{
    "error": "Text is required and must be a string"
}
const express = require("express");const app = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;
app.post("/api/text", (req, res) => {
  try {
    const { text } = req.body;
    if (!text || typeof text !== "string") {
      return res.status(400).json({
        error: "Text is required and must be a string",
      });      return res.status(400).json({
        error: "Text length cannot exceed 20 characters",
      });      message: `You said: ${text}`,  } catch (error) {
    console.error("Error processing request:", error);
    res.status(500).json({
      error: "Internal server error",
    });});  console.log(`Server is running on port ${PORT}`);
  console.log(`API endpoint: http://localhost:${PORT}/api/text`);
});

That’s a wrap for today! Found it useful? Hit follow and share it around. In the meantime, explore my top blogs — and I’ll catch you in the next one!

items.title: Getting Started with Terraform: A beginner’s Guide
items.author: Pravesh Sudha
items.content:
From Understanding Terraform to Creating a Nginx Server on EC2 instance, Everything made swfit

Hey there, welcome to the exciting world of Cloud and DevOps! If you’re new to this space, don’t worry — I’m here to guide you like a friend who’s just a step ahead. Today, we’re diving into , one of the coolest and most widely used Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools out there. Big companies like Adobe, Airbnb, and Red Hat rely on Terraform to manage their infrastructure using something called HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL). Sounds fancy, right? Don’t sweat it — we’ll break it all down!

In this blog, we’re going on a beginner-friendly journey. First, we’ll unpack what terms like , , and actually mean (no jargon overload, I promise). Then, we’ll peek under the hood to see how Terraform works its magic. Finally, we’ll roll up our sleeves and use Terraform to create an (a virtual server on AWS) with an to host a simple portfolio webpage. By the end, you’ll have a real, working project to show off!

Ready to jump in? Let’s get started without further ado!

Before we dive into the fun stuff, let’s make sure you’re set up for success. Since we’ll be using Amazon Web Services (AWS) as our cloud provider in this blog, you’ll need an . No worries if you don’t have one yet — it’s easy to create! Just head over to aws.amazon.com, sign up, and follow the steps to set up your free-tier account. This will let you experiment with Terraform without breaking the bank. Make sure you have your account ready, as we’ll be using it to spin up our EC2 instance and Nginx server later.

Psst — don’t forget to keep your AWS credentials safe! We’ll talk more about how to use them securely with Terraform soon.

💡 What is IaC, HCL, and Terraform? A Beginner’s Peek Under the Hood

Alright, let’s break this down like we’re chatting over coffee. Imagine you visit google.com and see that familiar search bar. You type a query, hit enter, and boom — results! On the surface, it’s just a website, but behind the scenes, it’s a whole different world. That webpage you’re seeing? It’s likely running on a Linux server tucked away in a data center somewhere across the globe. Pretty cool, right?

Now, most companies don’t want the hassle (or cost!) of building and maintaining their own data centers. Instead, they turn to cloud providers like , , or Google Cloud Platform (GCP). These providers let companies rent resources like (think virtual servers), (for files or databases), (to keep things safe), and (to connect everything). It’s like renting a fully equipped apartment instead of building a house from scratch!

So, how do companies set up these resources on the cloud? There are a few ways:

  1. : You log into the cloud provider’s website (like the AWS console) and manually click around to create servers, storage, or whatever you need. It’s straightforward but slow and prone to mistakes.
  2. : These are tools like (a Python library) that let you write code to create resources programmatically. It’s faster than clicking but requires coding skills.
  3. Infrastructure as Code (IaC): This is where things get really slick, and it’s what we’re focusing on today!

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is like writing a recipe for your cloud setup. Instead of manually creating resources, you write code that describes exactly what you want — servers, networks, databases, you name it. This code is stored in files, so you can reuse, share, or even version it (like tracking changes in a Google Doc). The big win? It reduces errors, saves time, and makes your infrastructure repeatable and predictable.

Now, let’s talk about  — HashiCorp Configuration Language. Think of HCL as the special language used to write these “recipes” for Terraform. It looks a bit like JSON (if you’ve seen that before), but it’s designed specifically for Terraform. HCL is a , which means you describe you want (e.g., “I want a server with 2 CPUs and 4GB of RAM”) rather than to build it step-by-step. Terraform takes your HCL code and figures out how to make it happen on your cloud provider. Pretty neat, huh?

Finally, let’s meet the star of the show: . It’s the most popular IaC tool out there, created by . With Terraform, you can write HCL code to provision and manage cloud resources — like spinning up a server or setting up a database — in a safe and efficient way. Here’s why it’s awesome:

  • : Since everything is defined in code, you’re less likely to mess things up by clicking the wrong button.
  • : You can track changes to your infrastructure like you would with code in Git.
  • : Want to recreate the same setup in another environment? Just run the same code!

Terraform works with tons of cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP, and more), so it’s super versatile. In this blog, we’ll use it to create an AWS EC2 instance and set up an Nginx server to host a portfolio webpage. Trust me, it’s going to be fun!

Okay, let’s get into the nitty-gritty of how Terraform actually does its magic! Don’t worry, I’ll keep it simple and break it down step-by-step so it feels like we’re just chatting about a cool tool. Terraform is all about turning your infrastructure dreams (like servers or storage) into reality by reading your code and talking to your cloud provider. Here’s how it works, in a way that even a beginner can follow:

Terraform reads you write (in HCL, remember?) that describe the of your infrastructure — like “I want one AWS EC2 server with Nginx installed.” Then, it uses (think of them as translators) to talk to the cloud provider’s APIs (like AWS’s) to create, update, or delete resources to match what you described. Let’s walk through the process:

This is where you tell Terraform what you want. You write HCL code to define your infrastructure’s  — like setting up an AWS EC2 instance, a storage bucket, or security roles. For example, you might say, “Hey, I need a virtual machine with 2 CPUs and a firewall rule.” Your code can include all sorts of resources: virtual machines, storage, networking, you name it!

Terraform also has this cool feature called . Think of modules like reusable templates (similar to Python modules). They let you package your code so you can reuse it across projects, keeping things organized and saving you time.

Once you’ve written your HCL code, you kick things off with the terraform init command. This sets up your project by:

  • Downloading the (like the AWS plugin if you’re using AWS) so Terraform can talk to your cloud provider.
  • Setting up the (more on this later) location. You can store this file remotely, like in an AWS S3 bucket, to keep it safe and share it with your team.

Next up is the terraform plan command. This is like Terraform’s “preview mode.” It compares your HCL code (what you ) with the current state of your infrastructure (what you ), which is tracked in the . Then, it shows you a plan of what it’ll do—like “I’ll create one EC2 instance and a security group.” This step is super helpful because it lets you double-check everything before making changes, avoiding any “oops, I didn’t mean to delete that!” moments.

When you’re ready to make things happen, you run terraform apply. This command takes the plan from the previous step and tells Terraform to go for it! It talks to your cloud provider’s API (like AWS) to create, update, or delete resources to match your HCL code. For example, it might spin up that EC2 instance or configure a storage bucket exactly as you specified.

Need to clean up? The terraform destroy command is your friend. It reads the , figures out all the resources Terraform is managing, and deletes them in the right order (taking dependencies into account so nothing breaks). It’s like hitting the reset button on your infrastructure.

Key Terraform Terms to Know

Let’s quickly cover some words you’ll hear a lot when working with Terraform:

  • : These are like plugins that let Terraform talk to different platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP, etc.). For example, the AWS provider knows how to interact with AWS’s APIs to create resources.
  • : This is a file (usually called terraform.tfstate) that keeps track of the current state of your infrastructure. It’s like Terraform’s memory, storing details about what resources exist. Storing it in a remote location (like an S3 bucket) is a good idea to avoid accidental changes and make it easier to collaborate.
  • : These are reusable chunks of Terraform code. Just like Python modules, they help you organize your code and reuse it across projects, making your life way easier.

💡 How to Write Code in HCL?

Alright, let’s talk about writing code in HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL) — don’t worry, it’s way simpler than it sounds! HCL is the language Terraform uses, and it’s designed to be easy to read and write, even for beginners. Think of it like writing a clear set of instructions for Terraform to follow. In HCL, you’ll work with a few key concepts: , , and . Let’s break them down and look at some examples to make it crystal clear!

Resources: Building Blocks of Your Infrastructure

are the actual pieces of infrastructure you want to create or manage — like virtual machines, storage buckets, security roles, databases, or even serverless functions. Each resource is defined in HCL with a specific type and configuration. For example, if you want to create an AWS EC2 instance, you’d use the aws_instance resource.

Here’s an example of creating a in AWS:

resource "aws_instance" "example" {  ami           = "ami-0c94855ba95c71c99"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  tags = {
    Name = "example-instance"
  }
  • resource "aws_instance" "example" tells Terraform you’re creating an EC2 instance named “example.”
  • ami specifies the Amazon Machine Image (think of it as the OS template for your server).
  • instance_type = "t2.micro" sets the size of the server (t2.micro is a small, free-tier option).
  • tags adds labels to help you identify the instance later.

Variables: Making Your Code Flexible

(or “vars” in HCL) are like placeholders that make your code reusable and dynamic. Instead of hardcoding values, you define them once in a variable and use them wherever needed. Variables can hold different types of data, like strings, numbers, booleans, lists, maps, objects, tuples, or sets.

Here’s an example where we use variables to define the AWS region and instance tags:

variable "region" {  description = "AWS region to deploy resources"  default     = "us-east-1"
variable "instance_tags" {
  description = "Tags for the EC2 instance"
  type        = map(string)
  default = {    Project     = "MyWebApp"}
resource "aws_instance" "example" {
  ami           = "ami-0c94855ba95c71c99"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  region        = var.region
  tags          = var.instance_tags
}
  • variable "region" defines a variable for the AWS region, with a default value of us-east-1.
  • variable "instance_tags" defines a map (key-value pairs) for tags, with defaults for Environment and Project.
  • In the aws_instance resource, var.region and var.instance_tags pull in those variable values, making the code flexible (you can change the region or tags without rewriting the resource).

Data Sources: Fetching External Info

let Terraform grab information from outside your configuration — like details about existing resources or dynamic data from your cloud provider. This is super useful when you need to work with resources that already exist or fetch the latest info, like the ID of the most recent Amazon Linux AMI.

Here’s an example of using a data source to fetch the latest Amazon Linux AMI:

data "aws_ami" "latest_amazon_linux" {  most_recent = true  filter {    values = ["amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2"]}
resource "aws_instance" "example" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.latest_amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
}
  • data "aws_ami" "latest_amazon_linux" tells Terraform to find the latest Amazon Linux AMI owned by Amazon.
  • most_recent = true ensures you get the newest version.
  • The filter narrows it down to AMIs with names matching amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2.
  • In the aws_instance resource, data.aws_ami.latest_amazon_linux.id uses the fetched AMI ID, so you don’t have to hardcode it.

Using resources, variables, and data sources in HCL makes your Terraform code powerful yet simple. Resources define you want, variables make it , and data sources keep it . Together, they let you build infrastructure that’s easy to manage and adapt.

💡 Practical Demo: Deploying an Nginx Server on AWS with Terraform

Alright, it’s time to roll up our sleeves and get hands-on! In this section, we’re going to use Terraform to deploy an on an AWS EC2 instance and host a simple portfolio webpage. Don’t worry if this sounds like a lot — we’ll go step-by-step, and by the end, you’ll have a live webpage you can show off! Let’s dive in.

Step 1: Install the AWS CLI

First, we need the AWS Command Line Interface (CLI) to connect our local machine to AWS. If you’re using , run these commands in your terminal:

curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"unzip awscliv2.zip

This downloads, unzips, and installs the AWS CLI. If you’re on a different OS, check the AWS CLI installation guide for the right commands.

Step 2: Set Up an AWS IAM User

To let Terraform talk to AWS, we need an with the right permissions. Here’s how to set it up:

  • Search for and click on > .
  • Give the user a name, like terra-user.
  • Under permissions, choose Attach permissions directly and select (this lets the user manage EC2 instances).
  • Review and create the user.
  • Select the terra-user in the IAM console, go to , and click .
  • Choose as the use case, then create the access key. Copy the and (you’ll need these soon!).

Now, let’s configure the AWS CLI with those credentials. In your terminal, run:

You’ll be prompted to enter:

  • : Paste the one you copied.
  • : Paste this too.
  • : Enter us-east-1.
  • : Enter json.

This sets up your AWS CLI to work with your terra-user account.

Step 3: Install Terraform

Next, we need on your system. For , run these commands:

sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y gnupg software-properties-commonwget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | gpg --dearmor | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg > /dev/null
echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(grep -oP '(?<=UBUNTU_CODENAME=).*' /etc/os-release || lsb_release -cs) main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list
sudo apt update
sudo apt-get install terraform

Step 4: Set Up the Project

Let’s create a project folder to organize our Terraform files:

mkdir basic-terracd basic-terra

Step 5: Create a User Data Script

We’ll create a script to install and set up our portfolio webpage. Inside the basic-terra directory, create a file called userdata.sh with this content:

#!/bin/bash
## Change the Name and Links Accordingly
# Update the package list and install Nginx
apt update -ysystemctl enable nginx
# Create portfolio content
cat <<EOF > /var/www/html/index.html<html lang="en">  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Pravesh Sudha | Portfolio</title>
  <style>      font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
      background-color: #f4f4f4;
      text-align: center;    }      color: #333;    p {      color: #666;    a {      text-decoration: none;  </style><body>
  <h1>Hi, I'm Pravesh Sudha</h1>
  <p>DevOps · Cloud · Content Creator</p>    <a href="https://blog.praveshsudha.com" target="_blank">Blog</a> |
    <a href="https://x.com/praveshstwt" target="_blank">Twitter</a> |
    <a href="https://www.youtube.com/@pravesh-sudha" target="_blank">YouTube</a> |
    <a href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/pravesh-sudha/" target="_blank">LinkedIn</a>
  </p></html>

This script installs Nginx, starts it, and creates a simple portfolio webpage at /var/www/html/index.html.

Step 6: Write Terraform Configuration

Now, let’s create the Terraform files to define our infrastructure.

terraform {  required_providers {      source = "hashicorp/aws"    }}  # Configuration options
  • Create a main.tf file to define the EC2 instance, security group, and output:
resource "aws_security_group" "sg" {  name        = "Basic-Security Group"
  description = "Allow port 80 for HTTP"
  tags = {    }resource "aws_vpc_security_group_egress_rule" "example" {
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.sg.id
  cidr_ipv4   = "0.0.0.0/0"}
resource "aws_vpc_security_group_ingress_rule" "example" {
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.sg.id
  cidr_ipv4   = "0.0.0.0/0"
  from_port   = 80  to_port     = 80resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-020cba7c55df1f615"  # Change the AMI accordingly
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  security_groups = [aws_security_group.sg.name]
  user_data     = file("userdata.sh")    Name = "basic-terra"}
output "instance_public_ip" {
  value       = aws_instance.web.public_ip
  description = "Website is running on this address:"
}

: The AMI ID (ami-020cba7c55df1f615) is region-specific and may change. To find the correct AMI for Ubuntu in us-east-1:

  • Go to the AWS Console > EC2 Dashboard > .
  • Select (e.g., Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS).
  • Copy the AMI ID displayed there and update the ami field in main.tf.

Step 7: Deploy with Terraform

Now, let’s bring it all to life! In the basic-terra directory, run these commands one by one:

This initializes the project and downloads the AWS provider plugin.

This shows you what Terraform will do (like creating an EC2 instance and security group). Double-check the plan to make sure it looks right.

terraform apply --auto-approve

Type yes when prompted. Terraform will create the resources, and after a few minutes, it’ll output the of your EC2 instance.

Step 8: See Your Portfolio in Action!

Copy the public IP address from the output, paste it into your browser (e.g., http://<public-ip>), and voilà! You should see your portfolio webpage running on Nginx, complete with your name and links to your blog, Twitter, YouTube, and LinkedIn. Congrats—you just built infrastructure with Terraform!

  • Set up an EC2 instance with an Ubuntu AMI.
  • Configure a security group to allow HTTP traffic (port 80).
  • Install Nginx and deploy a portfolio webpage using a user data script.
  • Output the public IP to access your site.

Wow, what a journey! You’ve just taken your first steps into the world of and Infrastructure as Code (IaC), and look at what you accomplished! From understanding the basics of HCL and Terraform to deploying a live on AWS with your very own portfolio webpage, you’ve got some serious DevOps skills to brag about now. We covered how Terraform works under the hood, wrote HCL code to define resources, and used it to spin up an EC2 instance like a pro.

Before we wrap up, let’s make sure you avoid any surprise AWS bills. Run this command in your basic-terra directory to clean up all the resources we created:

terraform destroy --auto-approve

This will delete your EC2 instance, security group, and anything else Terraform managed. Always double-check your AWS Console to confirm everything’s gone!

I hope this guide sparked your curiosity about Terraform and DevOps. Keep experimenting, building, and learning — there’s so much more to explore! If you want to connect or dive deeper into cloud and DevOps, feel free to follow me on my socials:

Thanks for following along, and happy coding! What’s next? Let me know if you want to tackle another project or dive deeper into Terraform!

items.title: How to increase the size of the swap in Ubuntu
items.author: mobin shaterian
items.content:
To increase the swap size in Ubuntu, you begin by disabling the current swap with sudo swapoff -a, optionally, removing the existing swap file if you plan to replace it. Then, a new swap file is created, typically fallocate for speed (sudo fallocate -l 40G /swapfile) or dd for broader compatibility. Once the file is created, it must be secured with the correct permissions (chmod 600) to ensure only the root can access it. Next, the file is formatted as swap using mkswap and enabled with swapon.

To make the change persistent after reboot, you need to edit the /etc/fstab file and add the line /swapfile none swap sw 0 0. This ensures the swap file is automatically activated on boot. You can verify that the new swap size is active using commands like swapon --show or free -h. This approach is effective for improving system performance, especially on low-memory machines or when running memory-intensive applications, without needing to repartition your disk.

Resize the existing swap file

2. Remove Old Swap File (if you want to replace it

3. Create a New 40 GB Swap File

sudo fallocate -l 40G /swapfilesudo dd if=/dev/zero of=/swapfile bs=1G count=40
sudo nano /etc/fstab/swapfile none swap sw 0 0

items.title: CacheBolt v0.7.1 — Ultrafast In-Memory & Multi-Cloud Cache for Microservices
items.author: Matías Salinas
items.content:
CacheBolt v0.7.1 — Ultrafast In-Memory & Multi-Cloud Cache for Microservices

is an ultrafast in-memory and multi-cloud cache and reverse proxy designed for microservices and high-traffic backend services. CacheBolt sits transparently between clients and your backend, delivering sub-millisecond response times when serving data from memory, and ensuring reliability through persistent object storage in multiple clouds or local disk.

By combining rapid in-memory caching with resilient multi-cloud storage, CacheBolt is a practical solution for teams seeking to:

  • Accelerate microservices and distributed systems with minimal changes
  • Offload repeated backend queries, saving resources and cost
  • Maintain service availability even when some dependencies are degraded
  • Simplify cache management and visibility with a built-in web interface

Version brings enhancements in memory efficiency, observability, and multi-cloud integration, continuing to improve CacheBolt for production-grade environments.

  • powered by Axum and Tokio
  • Concurrent in-memory caching with LRU eviction for optimal speed
  • Multi-cloud persistent storage:
  • Memory-based cache eviction configurable by system threshold
  • policies per path using regex rules
  • from persistent cache when upstreams are slow or unavailable
  • Integrated web admin interface for inspection and cache management
  • for full observability

📦 Quick Start & Downloads

Precompiled binaries for the most popular platforms are available on GitHub Releases:

./cachebolt --config ./config/prod.yaml
docker run --rm -p 3000:3000 \  -v $(pwd)/config:/config \  -e GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=/config/adc.json \
  ghcr.io/msalinas92/cachebolt:latest \
  --config /config/config.yaml

🖥️ Web-Based Admin Interface

CacheBolt comes with a lightweight bundled directly into the binary — no additional setup or frontend required.

  • Inspecting current in-memory cache entries in real-time
  • Clearing memory or persistent cache on demand
  • Monitoring cache sizes, TTL, and resource usage
  • All fully static and embedded for easy deployment

CacheBolt is designed to maximize cache efficiency and system reliability through a clear flow:

Client sends GET request        |proxy_handler receives request        v
Check if URI is marked as degraded (should_failover)
        |
        ├── Yes --> try_cache(key)
        │            ├── Hit in memory? 
        │            │     └── ✅ Serve from memory
        │            ├── Else: Hit in storage?
        │            │     └── ✅ Load from selected storage backend
        │            │            └── Load into memory + Serve
        │            └── Else: ❌ Return 502 (no cache, no backend)
        │             |      Check MEMORY_CACHE for key             ├── Hit --> ✅ Serve from memory                  |         Acquire semaphore (concurrency guard)                  ├── Denied --> Check memory again
                  │               ├── Hit --> ✅ Serve
                  │               └── ❌ Return 502 (overloaded)                  └── Acquired --> forward_request to backend                                   ├── Response latency > threshold?
                                   │         └── Yes --> mark_latency_fail
                                   |                                   │         |
                                   │         ├── Build CachedResponse
                                   │         ├── In failover mode?
                                   │         │     ├── Yes --> Skip caching
                                   │         │     └── No:
                                   │         │           ├── Put in MEMORY_CACHE
                                   │         │           └── Send to CACHE_WRITER (persist to backend)
                                   │         └── ✅ Return response
                                   |
                                   └── Downstream failed --> try_cache fallback

Probabilistic Cache Refreshing

To avoid stale data, CacheBolt supports . You can specify a percentage of requests that will bypass cache and fetch a fresh copy from your backend:

cache:  refresh_percentage: 10

In this configuration, approximately 1 in 10 requests for the same cache key will force a refresh from the upstream service, keeping your data up to date with minimal impact on performance or backend load.

Configuration is done via YAML. Here’s a practical example:

app_id: my-serviceproxy_port: 3000max_concurrent_requests: 200
downstream_base_url: http://localhost:4000
storage_backend: s3s3_bucket: my-cachebolt-bucket
azure_container: cachebolt-container
cache:
  memory_threshold: 80    # % of system memory usage before evicting
  refresh_percentage: 10  # Probability of forced refresh per key  default_max_latency_ms: 3000    - pattern: "^/api/v1/products/.*"    - pattern: "^/auth/.*"ignored_headers:  - if-none-match

🔐 Authentication for Cloud Storage

Google Cloud Storage (GCS):

export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="/path/to/service-account.json"
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your-access-key-idexport AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your-secret-key
export AWS_REGION=us-east-1
export AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT=your_account_nameexport AZURE_STORAGE_ACCESS_KEY=your_access_key

No credentials required. Cache files are stored locally.

CacheBolt exposes a /metrics endpoint (default port 3000) with Prometheus-compatible metrics:

  • cachebolt_proxy_requests_total{uri}
  • cachebolt_downstream_failures_total{uri}
  • cachebolt_rejected_due_to_concurrency_total{uri}
  • cachebolt_failover_total{uri}
  • cachebolt_memory_hits_total{uri}
  • cachebolt_memory_store_total{uri}
  • cachebolt_memory_fallback_hits_total
  • cachebolt_proxy_request_latency_ms{uri}
  • cachebolt_latency_exceeded_ms{uri}
  • cachebolt_latency_exceeded_total{uri}
  • cachebolt_persist_attempts_total{backend}
  • cachebolt_persist_errors_total{backend}
  • cachebolt_persistent_fallback_hits_total
  • cachebolt_fallback_miss_total

This allows teams to monitor cache efficiency, backend health, and system performance with ease.

You can clear both the in-memory and persistent cache layers via a single endpoint:

curl -X DELETE "http://localhost:3001/admin/cache?backend=true"
  • Setting backend=true will trigger deletion from all configured persistent storage backends (S3, GCS, Azure, or local).

📊 Memory Cache Status Endpoint

Inspect the state of the in-memory cache in real time with:

curl --location 'http://localhost:3001/admin/status-memory'

This returns metadata for each entry, including cache key, insertion time, size, and TTL remaining.

CacheBolt continues to evolve to meet the needs of modern service architectures — combining the speed of in-memory caching with the robustness of multi-cloud persistence, all in a single, easy-to-deploy binary.

items.title: How to Build a Simple URL Shortener with .NET 9 Minimal API?
items.author: Şafak AKÇA
items.content:
How to Build a Simple URL Shortener with .NET 9 Minimal API?

In this article, I want to show you how to build a simple URL shortener using .

First, what is url shortener and why we need it?

The url shortener is convert long url to short version, there are some tools for that, but we’ll coding an example manually.

We need it because short links are easier to read, share, and remember – especially in messages or on social media.It will be a basic project, and we will write it step by step.

Step 1 — Create a New Minimal API Project

First, we need to create a new .NET project.You can open your terminal and run this command:

dotnet new web -n UrlShortener

This command creates a new minimal API project called .Now go to that folder:

Step 2 — Add Swagger (OpenAPI)

Swagger helps us test the API easily.We need to add it in Program.cs file like this:

using System.Security.Cryptography;using System.Text;
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);
builder.Services.AddOpenApi();
var app = builder.Build();
if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())    app.MapOpenApi();
    app.UseSwaggerUI(options =>
    {
        options.SwaggerEndpoint("/openapi/v1.json", "OpenAPI V1");
    });

Step 3 — Create a Shorten URL Endpoint

Let’s create a method that takes a long URL and gives back a short one.Add this code to your Program.cs file:

var urlMap= new Dictionary<string, string>();
app.MapPost("/shorten", (string longUrl) =>
{
    var shortCode = GenerateShortCode();
    urlMap[shortCode] = longUrl;
    return Results.Ok($"http://localhost:5013/{shortCode}");
});

Step 4 — Redirect to the Long URL

Now let’s add another endpoint.When someone sends a short code, it will find the original long URL and send them there. Add this code to your Program.cs file:

app.MapGet("/{code}", (string code) =>{
    if (urlMap.TryGetValue(code, out var longUrl))
    {
        return Results.Redirect(longUrl);
    }
    return Results.NotFound("Short URL not found");
});

Step 5 — Generate Short Code Function

Let’s write a simple function to generate random codes.Add this function to your Program.cs file:

string GenerateShortCode(){
    char[] Base32Chars = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPRSTUVWXYZ0123456789".ToCharArray();
    var bytes = new byte[8];
    RandomNumberGenerator.Fill(bytes);
    var key = new StringBuilder(8);
    for (int i = 0; i < bytes.Length; i++)
    {
        key.Append(Base32Chars[bytes[i] % Base32Chars.Length]);
    }}

Step 6 — Run the Project and Test

Open your browser and go to:

http://localhost:5000/swagger
  1. Use POST /shorten and send:
{  "longUrl": "https://www.google.com"

2. You will get something like:

http://localhost:5013/ABCD1234

Open that short URL → It will redirect you to Google!

Right now, our URL shortener works well for learning and testing.But in a real-world project, we need to think about , , and .

Here are the next steps I’m planning:

  • : Same long URL should return the same short code.
  • : Use a smart hashing algorithm and avoid scanning all data.
  • : Save the data with unique indexes for fast lookups.
  • : Keep the most used URLs in memory to speed up redirection.

I hope the article was useful for you..

items.title: AI-Powered Pipeline Linting: How I Built a GPT Assistant for My CI/CD Workflows
items.author: Sourojit Das
items.content:
It started with a merge request that looked fine.

✅ Tests passed✅ Code reviewed

I scrolled through the CI config and noticed this:

# security-scan:#   runs-on: ubuntu-latest#     - run: ./scan.sh

The security scan job was commented out.It wasn’t an accident — just forgotten.

No one caught it because the syntax was fine, and the pipeline still ran.

It didn’t fail — it just… didn’t protect anything.

🤕 The Real Problem: We Lint Syntax, Not Intent

YAML linters are great at catching:

    But they’ll happily accept a CI pipeline that:

    • Pushes untagged Docker images
    • Runs only on main instead of release/*

    The structure is valid — but the .

    A DevOps-aware reviewer that reads your pipeline like a human and says:“Hey… you sure about this?”

    🤖 The Idea: Ask GPT to Review My CI

    So I built a script — ci-lint.py — that sends a pipeline YAML file to GPT-4 (or 3.5) and asks for a human-style sanity check.

    Plain-English feedback like this:

    - The 'build' job uses an unpinned image (`node:latest`). Consider pinning for consistency.- The 'security-scan' step is commented out. If this was unintentional, re-enable it.
    - No caching is set up for dependencies.

    Not bad for an AI that’s never seen my repo.

    🧪 Building ci-lint.py: A Narrated Walkthrough

    • Feed it into a GPT prompt that asks for issues
    • Print the bullet-point response
    import openaiimport sysimport yaml
    openai.api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
        print("Usage: python ci-lint.py <ci-file.yml>")with open(filename, 'r') as f:
    parsed_yaml = yaml.safe_load(content)
    You are a DevOps assistant helping developers improve their CI/CD pipeline.
    Here is a CI configuration file in YAML. Please review it and provide feedback on:
    - Potential security risks (e.g., unpinned Docker images)
    - Missing best practices (e.g., no test coverage or caching)
    - Anything suspicious or incomplete (e.g., commented-out jobs)
    Respond with 3–6 clear, actionable bullet points.
    {content}"""
    response = openai.ChatCompletion.create(
        model="gpt-4",  # or gpt-3.5-turbo        {"role": "system", "content": "You help developers improve CI/CD pipelines."},
            {"role": "user", "content": prompt}
        ]
    print("\n📋 GPT-CI Linter Report:\n")
    print(response.choices[0].message.content.strip())

    🧠 What GPT Actually Caught

    Here’s what it told me after scanning a typical GitHub Actions workflow:

    jobs:  build:    steps:
          - uses: actions/checkout@v3
          - run: npm install      # security-scan:
    - The Docker image used is `ubuntu-latest`, which may change. Pin a specific version for consistency.- The 'security-scan' job is commented out. If this was unintentional, re-enable it.
    - No caching is configured for npm dependencies.
    - There is no test step — consider adding `npm test` to catch regressions.

    It flagged things that a human might notice during code review — but in this case, no one did.

    🤝 Making It CI-Native: Run in GitHub Actions

    Once I saw it worked locally, I wanted to run it on .

    Here’s a basic GitHub Action to do that:

    # .github/workflows/ai-ci-lint.ymlname: GPT CI Linter  pull_request:      - ".github/workflows/*.yml"  gpt-ci-lint:    steps:        uses: actions/checkout@v3        uses: actions/setup-python@v4          python-version: 3.10
          - name: Install dependencies
            run: pip install openai pyyaml        env:
              OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
            run: |
              python ci-lint.py .github/workflows/ci.yml

    🧭 Where This Actually Helped

    • It flagged an unpinned node:latest image
    • It reminded me to cache pip and npm folders
    • It caught when I pushed to main without triggering test jobs
    • It noticed a commented-out scan.sh job that intentional, but wasn’t

    It’s not a replacement for code review — it’s an AI-powered second set of eyes that never gets tired of reading YAML.

    ⚠️ It’s Not Perfect (But It’s Honest)

    • GPT might hallucinate suggestions
    • It can’t understand your internal security policies
    • It’s only as smart as the prompt and the config you give it

    But as a ?

    • Add coverage for GitLab CI, CircleCI, Bitbucket
    • Build a repo with common prompts + workflows
    • Extend to .dockerfile, .env, and Helm charts
    • Add # gpt-ci-ignore to skip specific jobs

    If you’re already automating tests, builds, and deploys — Why not automate a little wisdom too?

    Let GPT give your pipeline a sanity check.It won’t catch everything — but it just might catch the thing that costs you production.

    Want the code? DM me or comment — I’ll publish the starter repo soon.

    Let me know how GPT helps your pipeline make fewer dumb mistakes.

items.title: Five Great DevOps Job Opportunities
items.author: Mike Vizard

items.title: Streamline DevOps troubleshooting: Integrate CloudWatch investigations with Slack
items.author: Paige Broderick
items.content:
Infrastructure alerts pose a challenge for DevOps teams, particularly when they occur outside of regular business hours. The complexity isn’t merely in receiving notifications, it lies in rapidly assessing their severity and determining the root cause. This challenge is compounded when upstream service disruptions cascade into multiple downstream alerts, creating a confusion of notifications that mask the true source of the problem. DevOps teams find themselves working backwards through a complex web of interconnected services, unsure whether to start investigating at the application, network, or infrastructure level.

To reduce resolution time and alert root cause analysis, AWS introduced CloudWatch Investigations, a generative AI-powered capability within Amazon CloudWatch. Powered by Amazon Q Developer, a generative AI–powered assistant for software development, CloudWatch investigations analyzes multiple metrics, logs, and deployment events to provide suggestions for remediation and root-cause analyses, reducing alarm resolution time. A key advantage of this feature is the ability to integrate these findings directly into Microsoft Teams and Slack, making sure developers and stakeholders receive immediate alerts when issues arise. This centralized collaboration approach enables teams to work together efficiently, reducing duplicate efforts and facilitating consistent problem-solving across the organization.

In this blog post, we will walk through how to integrate CloudWatch Investigations with Slack channels and demonstrate how to interact with investigations in Slack.

CloudWatch Investigations can be started in multiple ways, like from existing Amazon CloudWatch log insights, metrics, or alarms. To demonstrate CloudWatch Investigations functionalities, we will use CloudWatch alarms in a sample web application available in the aws-samples GitHub repository. Steps on how to deploy this web app in your AWS environment, via a CloudFormation template, can be found here. You can learn more about the architecture of the resources deployed in the AWS One Observability workshop. If you choose to deploy the sample web application, you will be responsible for all service charges associated with the CloudFormation template deployment. Alternatively, you can use existing CloudWatch alarms in your environment. Examples of common Amazon CloudWatch alarms include: MemoryUtilization, CPUUtiliziation, 5xxErrors and 4xxError. A full list of available alarms can be found here.

For this blog, we will utilize a pre-configured alarm to monitor when one of the website services, backed by an Application Load Balancer, experiences abnormal response times. When the alarm triggers, CloudWatch Investigations automatically initiates an investigation, analyzing both the current alarm state and 90 days of CloudTrail event history to generate hypotheses and determine potential root causes. The investigation insights are published to a Slack channel via Amazon Q Developer in Chat Applications and Amazon Simple Notification Service (SNS).

Figure 1. Architecture diagram of the services involved in the investigation integration in Slack

  1. Launch the Amazon CloudFormation template associated with the One Observability lab outlined in the AWS Samples GitHub.
  2. Set up a Standard Amazon SNS topic by following the instructions outlined here. To enable CloudWatch investigations to send notifications to Slack, you must add an access policy to the Amazon SNS topic, an example can be found here.
  3. When the topic configuration is complete, navigate to Amazon Q Developer in Chat Applications (formerlyto configure the integration between Amazon Q and Slack by following the instructions outlined here. To allow channel members to interact with the investigation in Slack, add the following permission templates to the Channel role settings: Notification Permissions, Amazon Q Permissions, and Amazon Q Operations assistant permissions. More details on these permissions can be located here.

Setting up CloudWatch Investigations

Figure 2. Configure for this account button within the AWS Console

Before we can set up an investigation, we need to create an investigation group. This is an organizational structure to manage common properties of the investigation like retention requirements, encryption, access permissions and the SNS topic linked. Click Configure for this account and follow the prompts in the console to set up the investigation group. Detailed explanations for each prompt are located in the documentation here. For this demo, we left the default options for steps 1 and 2 of the prompts. In step 3, please select the existing SNS topic created in the prerequisites section.

Figure 3. Select SNS topic for Q Developer Operational Insights

For the investigation trigger, we will use an existing alarm created by the CloudFormation deployment mentioned at the beginning of this blog. The sample alarm is named:

ApplicationInsights/Services/AWS/ApplicationELB/TargetResponseTime/app/Servic-lista-... 

and it goes into state when one of the website services, backed by an Application Load Balancer, experiences abnormal response times.

To configure this alarm to automatically start an investigation when it goes into an state:

  1. In the CloudWatch console, choose ,
  2. Search for the alarm name and click on it
  3. Choose once to skip the metrics and conditions section
  4. Choose and then select your investigation group as outlined in figure 4
  5. Choose Skip to Preview and create, then choose

Figure 4. Configure alarm to automatically start investigations

At this point, we are ready to test the solution. To simulate a website traffic overload and trigger the alarm, we are going to use Amazon ECS tasks deployed as part of the sample web application. Open up CloudShell and run the following command:

PETLISTADOPTIONS_CLUSTER=$(aws ecs list-clusters | jq '.clusterArns[]|select(contains("PetList"))' -r)

TRAFFICGENERATOR_SERVICE=$(aws ecs list-services --cluster $PETLISTADOPTIONS_CLUSTER | jq '.serviceArns[]|select(contains("trafficgenerator"))' -r)

aws ecs update-service --cluster $PETLISTADOPTIONS_CLUSTER --service $TRAFFICGENERATOR_SERVICE --desired-count 5

The command will launch 5 instances of the Amazon ECS traffic generator container task. Once the tasks are running (after about 5 minutes), the ALB will become overloaded with requests, forcing the alarm into state as shown below. You should also see a new investigation created.

Figure 5. CloudWatch Alarm in ALARM state

Interacting with the investigation via Slack

Once the alarm is triggered, an investigation is initiated. Since we associated the investigation with an Amazon SNS topic and subscribed our Slack client to it, we can see a message in our Slack channel from Amazon Q as seen in figure 6.

Figure 6. Slack notification for open investigation

Within Slack, channel members can accept useful hypotheses and discard unhelpful ones by clicking on the button. They can also add text-based notes of observations or evidence to the investigation by clicking on the button. Amazon Q will respond to messages within the same thread as the original investigation message. Channel members will be able to track who has accepted or discarded messages, as well as notes made about the investigation. This emphasizes the power of Slack integration, as teams can collaborate on the investigation and track who is actively working on it. It is important to note that CloudWatch Investigations uses Generative AI and may provide suggestions different from those below based on your specific account environment.

Figure 7. Accept or discard investigation suggestions from Slack

When integrated with Slack, CloudWatch Investigations can provide suggestions and root-cause hypotheses. Channel members with appropriate permissions can access metrics, charts, and additional information related to the investigation by clicking the blue header at the top of the investigation message. This link will direct users to the CloudWatch Investigations feed in the AWS console as shown below in figure 8.

Figure 8. CloudWatch Investigations in CloudWatch console.

Integrating CloudWatch Investigations with Slack or Teams channels improves developers’ visibility of arising issues and provides targeted recommendations to reduce alarm resolution time. The and buttons make it straightforward to track who is actively working on an investigation, fostering a culture of collaboration. The best part? The integration is quick to set up, especially with existing alarms.

If you launched the CloudFormation template mentioned at the beginning of this blog, the services will continue to run unless you delete them. To make sure that you are not charged for use of the resources after the demo, please follow the below steps to delete the resources created as part of the steps performed on this blog.

  1. Remove the Amazon Q in Chat Applications Slack integration by clicking on Remove Workspace Integration and policy as explained here.
  2. Delete Amazon SNS topic and subscription as explained here.
  3. Remove the CloudWatch Investigations as explained here.
  4. Delete the images under the Amazon ECR repository named as explained here.
  5. Open the CloudShell console or AWS CLI and execute the two commands below:
curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aws-samples/one-observability-demo/main/PetAdoptions/cdk/pet_stack/resources/destroy_stack.sh | bash

aws cloudformation delete-stack –stack-name CDKToolkit

After executing the above command, the resources of the demo should be destroyed. Look at the CloudFormation console in case of potential errors.

The new CloudWatch Investigations feature reduces alarm resolution time for development teams by providing actionable insights and recommendations. It is straightforward to connect investigations to a team’s primary form of communication, such as Teams or Slack, to improve notification awareness and interaction. To learn more about the capabilities of CloudWatch Investigations check out the feature announcement and documentation.
